input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Digital Twin** in the provided documents primarily focuses on **cyber-biophysical systems** (Document [7]) and **automotive systems** (Documents [8], [9], [14], [17]), with extensions to **aerospace applications** (Document [16]) and **battery systems** (Document [17]). The **physical twin** is characterized as follows:

1. **Automotive Systems (Primary Domain)**:
   - **Type**: Connected vehicles, powertrain components (e.g., electric motors, battery systems), and manufacturing assembly lines.
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Vehicle-level**: Sensors (e.g., LiDAR, radar, IMUs), actuators, and embedded control units (ECUs) for real-time data acquisition.
     - **Powertrain**: High-voltage battery packs (e.g., Li-ion), inverters, and motor controllers with bidirectional communication interfaces (e.g., CAN, LIN, Ethernet).
     - **Manufacturing**: Robotic arms, CNC machines, and assembly cells with IoT-enabled sensors for condition monitoring.
   - **Operational Domain**:
     - **On-road**: Real-time telemetry (speed, acceleration, energy consumption) and over-the-air (OTA) updates for adaptive control.
     - **Off-road**: Simulation of extreme environmental conditions (e.g., temperature extremes, vibration) for durability testing.
     - **Manufacturing**: Predictive maintenance of assembly lines to minimize downtime.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Dynamic Interoperability**: Seamless integration with **ISO 23247-compliant** digital twin frameworks (Documents [10], [14], [16]) for bidirectional data synchronization.
     - **Cyber-Physical Coupling**: Real-time synchronization between physical sensors (e.g., voltage/current sensors in batteries) and virtual models via **edge computing** (Document [14]).
     - **Multi-Scale Modeling**: From **microscopic** (e.g., battery cell degradation) to **macroscopic** (e.g., vehicle fleet performance) (Document [9]).
     - **Regulatory Compliance**: Alignment with **ISO/IEC 30173** (Digital Twin terminology) and **ISO 23247-1** (manufacturing framework) for standardization (Documents [10], [11]).
     - **Energy-Aware Design**: Optimization of energy consumption in electric vehicles (EVs) using **AI-driven simulation** (Document [15]).

2. **Aerospace Systems (Extended Domain)**:
   - **Type**: On-orbit satellites and space debris detection systems (Document [16]).
   - **Key Components**:
     - **Satellite Subsystems**: Attitude control systems, thermal management units, and collision avoidance sensors.
     - **Ground Segment**: Real-time telemetry processing and predictive modeling for debris avoidance maneuvers.
   - **Operational Domain**:
     - **Space Environment**: Simulation of orbital mechanics, radiation effects, and thermal cycling.
     - **Mission Criticality**: Adaptive control for collision avoidance using **ISO 23247-adapted frameworks** (Document [16]).

3. **Battery Systems (Specialized Domain)**:
   - **Type**: High-energy battery packs for EVs and grid storage (Document [17]).
   - **Key Components**:
     - **Cell-Level**: Li-ion cells with integrated health monitoring (e.g., State of Charge/State of Health sensors).
     - **Pack-Level**: Thermal management systems (e.g., liquid cooling loops) and balancing circuits.
   - **Operational Domain**:
     - **Lifetime Prediction**: AI-driven degradation modeling to estimate remaining useful life (RUL).
     - **Thermal Management**: Real-time optimization of cooling strategies to prevent thermal runaway.

4. **Cyber-Biophysical Systems (Theoretical Framework)**:
   - **Type**: Hybrid systems combining **cyber-physical** (e.g., IoT sensors) and **biophysical** (e.g., human-machine interfaces, biological sensors) components (Document [7]).
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Biological Integration**: Use of **wearable sensors** (e.g., EEG, ECG) for driver/operator health monitoring in automotive/aerospace contexts.
     - **Adaptive Control**: Machine learning models trained on **real-time physiological data** to adjust system parameters dynamically.

**Suitability for Digital Twinning**:
   - **Real-Time Data Requirements**: High-frequency sensor data (e.g., 100Hz for powertrain dynamics, 1Hz for battery health) enable closed-loop simulation.
   - **Model Complexity**: Multi-physics simulations (thermal, electrical, mechanical) require **high-fidelity digital replicas** (e.g., finite element analysis for battery degradation).
   - **Standardization**: Adherence to **ISO 23247** ensures interoperability across heterogeneous systems (e.g., automotive, aerospace, manufacturing).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** services in the provided documents are categorized into **real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, optimization, control, visualization, and adaptive learning**, tailored for **automotive, aerospace, and manufacturing domains**. Key services include:

1. **Real-Time Monitoring**:
   - **Service Type**: **Continuous telemetry and condition-based monitoring**.
   - **Target Users**: Fleet managers, manufacturing operators, and aerospace mission control teams.
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Automotive**:
       - Real-time **vehicle health diagnostics** (e.g., powertrain efficiency, battery degradation) via **edge-computed** sensor data (Document [14]).
       - **Predictive maintenance alerts** for assembly line equipment using **IoT sensor networks** (Document [9]).
     - **Aerospace**:
       - **On-orbit collision avoidance** by fusing satellite telemetry with **space debris tracking models** (Document [16]).
     - **Battery Systems**:
       - **State of Health (SoH) monitoring** with **AI-driven anomaly detection** (Document [17]).
   - **Technologies**:
     - **Protocols**: MQTT, OPC UA for real-time data streaming (implied by ISO 23247 compliance).
     - **Databases**: Time-series databases (e.g., InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) for sensor data storage (Document [14]).

2. **Predictive Analytics**:
   - **Service Type**: **Failure prediction, lifetime estimation, and scenario simulation**.
   - **Target Users**: Maintenance engineers, product designers, and regulatory bodies.
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Automotive**:
       - **Remaining Useful Life (RUL) prediction** for batteries using **machine learning models** trained on degradation data (Document [9]).
       - **Traffic-aware route optimization** for EVs to minimize energy consumption (Document [15]).
     - **Manufacturing**:
       - **Predictive maintenance** for CNC machines using **digital twin-driven fault detection** (Document [10]).
     - **Aerospace**:
       - **Orbital decay prediction** for satellites using **digital twin simulations** (Document [16]).
   - **Technologies**:
     - **Algorithms**: Random Forest, LSTM networks for time-series forecasting (Document [15]).
     - **Simulation Engines**: ANSYS, COMSOL for multi-physics modeling (implied by ISO 23247).

3. **Optimization**:
   - **Service Type**: **Energy efficiency, resource allocation, and process optimization**.
   - **Target Users**: Energy managers, supply chain planners, and aerospace mission operators.
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Automotive**:
       - **Thermal management optimization** for batteries to extend lifespan (Document [17]).
       - **Charging infrastructure optimization** for EV fleets using **digital twin simulations** (Document [14]).
     - **Manufacturing**:
       - **Production line balancing** to minimize cycle time (Document [10]).
     - **Aerospace**:
       - **Fuel consumption optimization** for satellite maneuvers (Document [16]).
   - **Technologies**:
     - **Optimization Tools**: MATLAB Optimization Toolbox, Pyomo for constraint-solving (Document [15]).
     - **Edge Computing**: Localized optimization on **NVIDIA Jetson** or **Intel Movidius** for low-latency decisions (Document [14]).

4. **Control**:
   - **Service Type**: **Closed-loop adaptive control and autonomous decision-making**.
   - **Target Users**: Autonomous vehicle systems, robotics, and aerospace autopilots.
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Automotive**:
       - **Adaptive cruise control** with real-time path optimization (Document [8]).
       - **Autonomous parking assistance** using **digital twin-driven collision avoidance** (Document [9]).
     - **Aerospace**:
       - **Autonomous collision avoidance** for satellites (Document [16]).
     - **Battery Systems**:
       - **Dynamic charging profiles** to extend battery life (Document [17]).
   - **Technologies**:
     - **Control Algorithms**: Model Predictive Control (MPC), PID tuning (Document [14]).
     - **Hardware**: FPGA-based real-time controllers for low-latency execution (implied by edge computing).

5. **Visualization**:
   - **Service Type**: **Interactive 3D/4D modeling, dashboards, and AR/VR integration**.
   - **Target Users**: Engineers, technicians, and end-users (e.g., drivers).
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Automotive**:
       - **3D powertrain simulation** for virtual testing (Document [8]).
       - **AR-based maintenance guides** for assembly line technicians (Document [9]).
     - **Aerospace**:
       - **3D orbital debris visualization** for collision risk assessment (Document [16]).
     - **Battery Systems**:
       - **Thermal mapping** of battery packs in real-time (Document [17]).
   - **Technologies**:
     - **Tools**: Unity, Unreal Engine for 3D visualization (Document [15]).
     - **AR/VR**: Microsoft HoloLens for immersive diagnostics (implied by automotive use cases).

6. **Adaptive Learning**:
   - **Service Type**: **Continuous model refinement via AI/ML and human-in-the-loop feedback**.
   - **Target Users**: Data scientists, system architects.
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Automotive**:
       - **Federated learning** for improving predictive models across fleets without sharing raw data (Document [15]).
       - **Reinforcement learning** for autonomous driving policies (Document [8]).
     - **Aerospace**:
       - **Online learning** for satellite trajectory optimization (Document [16]).
   - **Technologies**:
     - **AI Frameworks**: TensorFlow, PyTorch for model training (Document [15]).
     - **Data Fusion**: Bayesian networks for combining sensor and simulation data (Document [7]).

7. **Standardized Services (ISO 23247 Alignment)**:
   - **Service Type**: **Framework-agnostic digital twin services** for interoperability.
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Lifecycle Management**: From **conceptual design** to **retirement** (Document [10]).
     - **Digital Thread Integration**: Seamless data flow between **CAD, PLM, and MES systems** (Document [12]).
     - **Security**: Role-based access control (RBAC) and **blockchain for data integrity** (implied by ISO 23247).

**Service Delivery Models**:
   - **Cloud-Based**: Centralized DT services for fleet management (Document [14]).
   - **Edge-Based**: Localized DT services for low-latency control (e.g., autonomous vehicles) (Document [14]).
   - **Hybrid**: Combines cloud for analytics and edge for real-time control (Document [15]).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** implementation in the provided documents leverages a **multi-layered toolchain** encompassing **standards, simulation engines, edge/cloud computing, AI/ML frameworks, and specialized enablers** for automotive, aerospace, and manufacturing domains. Below is a structured breakdown:

---

### **1. Standards and Frameworks**
- **ISO 23247-1 (Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing)**:
  - **Role**: Provides **architecture guidelines** for DT implementation, including **data models, interfaces, and lifecycle management** (Documents [10], [14], [16]).
  - **Key Components**:
    - **Digital Twin Model (DTM)**: Standardized **data structure** for physical-twin synchronization (e.g., **STEP AP242** for manufacturing) (Document [10]).
    - **Digital Twin Interface (DTI)**: Defines **communication protocols** (e.g., **OPC UA, MQTT**) for real-time data exchange (Document [12]).
    - **Digital Twin Management (DTMgmt)**: Tools for **version control, access control, and compliance tracking** (Document [10]).

- **ISO/IEC 30173 (Digital Twin – Concepts and Terminology)**:
  - **Role**: Establishes **vocabulary and definitions** for DT components (e.g., **Digital Twin Instance, Digital Twin Model**) (Document [11]).
  - **Extension**: Adapted for **aerospace** (e.g., **on-orbit collision avoidance**) (Document [16]).

- **Systems of Twinned Systems (SoTS) Framework** (Document [13]):
  - **Role**: Enables **interoperability between multiple DTs** (e.g., **vehicle DT ↔ manufacturing DT ↔ supply chain DT**).
  - **Technologies**: Uses **ontology-driven data integration** (e.g., **OWL, RDF**) for semantic alignment (Document [13]).

---

### **2. Simulation Engines and Modeling Tools**
- **Multi-Physics Simulation**:
  - **ANSYS**: For **thermal, structural, and fluid dynamics** simulations (e.g., battery cooling, aerospace thermal management) (implied by ISO 23247 compliance).
  - **COMSOL**: Specialized for **electromagnetic and battery modeling** (Document [17]).
  - **MATLAB/Simulink**: **Real-time control prototyping** and **model predictive control (MPC)** for automotive/aerospace systems (Document [14]).

- **AI/ML-Driven Simulation**:
  - **Digital Twin + AI Integration**:
    - **Generative AI**: Used for **automated model generation** from sensor data (Document [15]).
    - **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**: For **autonomous decision-making** in vehicles and satellites (Document [8], [16]).
  - **Tools**:
    - **TensorFlow/PyTorch**: Training **neural networks** for predictive maintenance (Document [15]).
    - **Scikit-learn**: **Traditional ML** for feature extraction in sensor data (Document [9]).

---

### **3. Edge and Cloud Computing Platforms**
- **Edge Computing (Low-Latency Processing)**:
  - **Frameworks**:
    - **NVIDIA Jetson**: Deployed for **real-time DT services** in autonomous vehicles (Document [14]).
    - **Intel Edge Insights**: For **predictive analytics at the edge** (e.g., battery health monitoring).
  - **Protocols**:
    - **5G/6G**: Enables **ultra-low-latency communication** for DT control (implied by automotive/aerospace use cases).
    - **MQTT/SNMP**: For **resource-constrained IoT devices** in manufacturing (Document [12]).

- **Cloud Computing (Scalable Analytics)**:
  - **Platforms**:
    - **AWS IoT Core**: Hosts **digital twin dashboards** and **AI model training** (Document [14]).
    - **Microsoft Azure Digital Twins**: Provides **scalable DT instances** for fleet management (Document [8]).
  - **Databases**:
    - **InfluxDB/TimescaleDB**: Time-series storage for **sensor data** (Document [14]).
    - **PostgreSQL/NoSQL**: For **metadata and configuration management** (Document [10]).

---

### **4. Data Management and Integration**
- **Digital Twin Data Model (DTM)**:
  - **Data Formats**:
    - **JSON/Protobuf**: For **interoperable data exchange** between DT components (Document [12]).
    - **STEP AP242**: Standardized **CAD/CAM integration** for manufacturing DTs (Document [10]).
  - **Ontologies**:
    - **OWL/RDF**: Used for **semantic interoperability** in Systems of Twinned Systems (SoTS) (Document [13]).

- **Data Fusion and Processing**:
  - **Tools**:
    - **Apache Kafka**: For **real-time data streaming** between edge and cloud (Document [14]).
    - **Spark/Flink**: **Batch/stream processing** for large-scale DT analytics (Document [9]).
  - **AI/ML Pipelines**:
    - **TensorFlow Extended (TFX)**: For **MLOps in DT environments** (Document [15]).

---

### **5. Specialized Enablers**
- **Automotive-Specific Tools**:
  - **VectorCANoe**: **CAN bus simulation** for vehicle DT validation (Document [8]).
  - **dSPACE**: **Hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing** for powertrain control (Document [9]).

- **Aerospace-Specific Tools**:
  - **STK (Satellite Tool Kit)**: For **orbital mechanics simulation** in satellite DTs (Document [16]).
  - **ESA’s Digital Twin Toolbox**: **Space debris tracking integration** (Document [16]).

- **Battery-Specific Tools**:
  - **Battery Management Systems (BMS)**: **Real-time SoH monitoring** (Document [17]).
  - **Battery Simulation Tools**: **PSAT, BatterySim** for degradation modeling (Document [17]).

- **Security and Privacy**:
  - **Blockchain**: For **immutable audit logs** of DT data (implied by ISO 23247).
  - **Federated Learning**: **Privacy-preserving AI training** across multiple DTs (Document [15]).

---

### **6. Development and Deployment Tools**
- **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**:
  - **SysML**: For **system architecture modeling** in DT frameworks (Document [10]).
  - **Eclipse Papyrus**: **UML-based DT design** (Document [12]).

- **CI/CD for Digital Twins**:
  - **Jenkins/GitLab CI**: For **automated DT model updates** (Document [14]).
  - **Docker/Kubernetes**: **Containerization of DT services** for scalability (Document [14]).

---

### **7. Human-Machine Interaction (HMI) Tools**
- **Visualization**:
  - **Unity/Unreal Engine**: **Immersive 3D DT visualization** (Document [15]).
  - **Tableau/Power BI**: **Dashboards for DT analytics** (Document [8]).

- **AR/VR**:
  - **Microsoft HoloLens**: **Augmented reality for maintenance** (Document [9]).
  - **Meta Quest**: **Virtual training for aerospace DT operations** (Document [16]).

---
**Toolchain Summary**:
| **Layer**               | **Technologies/Tools**                                                                 | **Primary Use Case**                          |
|--------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| **Standards**            | ISO 23247, ISO/IEC 30173, SoTS Framework                                                 | Interoperability, lifecycle management       |
| **Simulation**          | ANSYS, COMSOL, MATLAB/Simulink, TensorFlow/PyTorch                                    | Multi-physics, AI-driven modeling             |
| **Edge/Cloud**          | NVIDIA Jetson, AWS IoT, Azure Digital Twins, InfluxDB                                 | Real-time control, scalable analytics         |
| **Data Management**      | Kafka, Spark, OWL/RDF, STEP AP242                                                      | Data fusion, semantic interoperability       |
| **Automotive/Aerospace** | VectorCANoe, dSPACE, STK, BatterySim                                                  | Domain-specific validation                    |
| **Security**            | Blockchain, Federated Learning                                                          | Privacy, auditability                         |
| **HMI**                 | Unity, HoloLens, Tableau                                                              | Visualization, training                       |
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces a **100 ms clock synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination, adhering to stricter vehicular application requirements despite hardware limitations. Temporal granularity is dictated by the ROS2-based implementation, where each Function Entity (FE) executes as an independent ROS2 package with nodes handling real-time data processing (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*). Latency requirements are implicitly constrained by the ROS2 middleware’s publish-subscribe model, with no explicit mention of service-level time scales beyond the 100 ms synchronization constraint. The system’s temporal coherence relies on shared time references across distributed hardware components hosting the *Physical Twin Entity*, *Digital Twin Entity*, and *User Entity* components.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system follows a **decentralized, entity-based architecture** with three primary multiplicities: the *Physical Twin Entity*, *Digital Twin Entity*, and *User Entity*, each deployed on distinct hardware components. This structure diverges from ISO 23247’s *Operational Management Entity (OME)* by consolidating resource-specific and general automotive functionalities (e.g., sensors, actuators, compute) under the *Physical Twin Entity*. The *User Entity* is further subdivided into two subcomponents (*3D Visualization* and *Control Panel*), reflecting a hierarchical multiplicity within the user-facing layer. No explicit mention of multiple Digital Twin instances or centralized coordination mechanisms exists; instead, the system emphasizes **distributed ownership of functionality** and **entity-specific responsibilities**, impacting engineering processes and inter-component coordination.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system’s orchestration adheres to the **ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA)**, with Function Entities (FEs) mapped to ROS2 packages and executed as independent nodes. The architecture employs an **entity-based decomposition**—*Physical Twin Entity* (sensors/actuators/compute), *Digital Twin Entity* (data management/control), and *User Entity* (visualization/control)—each deployed on separate hardware. Coordination is achieved via ROS2’s **publish-subscribe communication model**, enabling inter-FE data exchange and synchronization. Resource management is implicit within each entity’s scope, with no centralized orchestrator; instead, the system leverages ROS2’s **distributed execution environment** to manage dependencies and interoperability. Deviations from ISO 23247 include the *Physical Twin Entity*’s expanded role and the split *User Interface FE* into subcomponents, necessitating tailored engineering processes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247** standard, which defines an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** where the **Digital Twin Entity** represents Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs) such as equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes. The system employs **computational models** categorized as follows:
  - **Geometric Models**: Likely used for spatial representation of OMEs (e.g., CAD models of machinery or facilities).
  - **Behavioral Models**: Dynamically simulate OME operations, interactions, and process behaviors (e.g., finite element analysis or process simulation models).
  - **Analytical Models**: Support predictive analytics, optimization, or root-cause analysis (e.g., machine learning models for fault detection or performance forecasting).

  **Data sources** include real-time and historical data from OMEs, collected via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which interfaces with sensors, IoT devices, or PLCs. **Data management approaches** are not explicitly detailed in the documents, but the standard implies structured storage (e.g., relational databases, time-series databases) for OME-specific data. **Model relationships** are defined by the **Functional Entities (FEs)**, which modularize tasks such as data transformation, user interfacing, or OME actuation. The **Digital Twin Entity** integrates these FEs to maintain consistency between physical and digital representations.

  **Technologies** for implementation are not mandated by ISO 23247 but may include **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for interoperability between entities, enabling standardized data exchange and control protocols. The system’s **roles** are partitioned by the four entities (OME, Data Collection/Device Control, Digital Twin, and User), each with distinct responsibilities for data acquisition, processing, representation, and interaction.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **ISO 23247** standard implicitly addresses **fidelity and validity** through its **entity-based architecture**, which mandates alignment between the **Digital Twin Entity** and the **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**. While specific **fidelity requirements** (e.g., model accuracy thresholds) are not detailed, the standard emphasizes **validation and verification (V&V)** via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which ensures real-time data consistency. **Uncertainty sources** may arise from sensor noise, model simplifications, or environmental variations, though quantification methods are not specified. **Error detection and correction mechanisms** are likely embedded in the **Functional Entities (FEs)**, which may include anomaly detection algorithms or feedback loops for recalibration. **Quality assurance processes** are implied to be part of the **Digital Twin Entity’s** lifecycle management, ensuring models and data adhere to predefined standards (e.g., ISO 23247 compliance). However, explicit methodologies for uncertainty quantification or formal V&V protocols are not provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves real-time data acquisition from physical sensors and preprocessing before digital representation. **Data types transmitted** include raw LiDAR point clouds (2D/3D), vehicle state parameters (motor actuation, steering servo signals), and object tracks. **Preprocessing steps** are executed by the **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** SW component, which processes raw LiDAR data into structured object tracks and driving path projections. **Communication protocols** for sensor-to-DTS data transfer are not explicitly detailed, but the system relies on **ROS2** for internal SW communication and **Wi-Fi (UDP)** for inter-entity data propagation. **Transmission frequencies** are implied to be high (unspecified) to support real-time 3D visualization (e.g., Fig. 3a). **Data quality assurance** is inferred through the **Data Pre-Processing FE**, which likely includes noise filtering, alignment, and unit conversion via the **Data Translation** component (ISO 23247-compliant). **Data integration mechanisms** involve serialization and alignment of message types across heterogeneous data streams (e.g., LiDAR, motor/servo signals) before storage or visualization. **Event triggers** for data capture are tied to sensor polling (e.g., LiDAR scans) and vehicle state changes (e.g., motor/steering actuation).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this DTS is facilitated by the **Controlling FE (ACC)** component, which translates digital commands into physical actuation. **Command types** include **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control)** inputs, validated via the **Data Translation** component for unit conversion and serialization. **Control message formats** are standardized through ROS2 message types, transmitted over **Wi-Fi (UDP)** to the physical twin. **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are not explicitly described, but the **Data Translation FE** ensures message integrity via serialization and unit alignment. **Feedback systems** are implied through real-time **3D visualization** (Fig. 3a), where projected driving paths and object tracks provide situational awareness for control adjustments. **Conflict resolution** is not detailed, but the system architecture suggests centralized control via the **Controlling FE**, which likely prioritizes ACC commands over other inputs. **Command execution confirmation** is inferred via the **Actuation FE**, which executes motor/steering servo commands based on validated digital signals.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s Technical Connection** relies on a **hybrid network architecture** combining **ROS2** for intra-system communication and **Wi-Fi (UDP)** for inter-entity data exchange. **Communication protocols** include:
- **ROS2** (for SW components within the DTS, e.g., Data Translation, Visualization FE).
- **Wi-Fi (UDP)** (for inter-entity communication between Physical Twin, Digital Twin, and User Entities).
**Network topology** is not explicitly detailed, but the architecture implies a **client-server or peer-to-peer** model with centralized control via the **Controlling FE**. **Bandwidth and latency requirements** are critical for real-time 3D visualization and ACC, though specific metrics (e.g., Mbps, ms) are not provided. **Reliability mechanisms** are inferred through the **Data Translation FE**, which ensures data consistency via serialization and unit conversion. **Security protocols** are not mentioned, but UDP’s stateless nature suggests minimal built-in security (e.g., no TLS).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents. Key assumptions based on context:
- **Hosting platforms**: Likely **edge computing** (due to real-time requirements for ACC and 3D visualization) or **on-premise** (for localized deployment).
- **Computational requirements**: High-performance processing for **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking**, **Data Translation**, and **3D Visualization** (ROS2-based).
- **Storage requirements**: Data storage for raw LiDAR point clouds, processed tracks, and reports (via **Data Storage** FE).
- **Scalability mechanisms**: Not described, but ROS2’s modularity suggests horizontal scaling for additional sensors or users.
- **Deployment models**: Implied support for **distributed deployment** (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User Entity) with **Wi-Fi-based interconnection**.
- **Availability/reliability assurance**: Not detailed, but the **Data Translation FE** ensures data consistency, and **real-time visualization** (Fig. 3a) suggests redundancy is implied for critical components (e.g., ACC).
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The lifecycle of the Digital Twin (DT) system, as outlined in the **ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing** standard, is structured around an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** with distinct phases and transitions governed by functional and operational requirements. The lifecycle begins with the **development phase**, where **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**—such as equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes—are identified and mapped to their corresponding **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)**. Each OME is represented digitally via the DTE, which interacts with the physical system through **Data Collection and Device Control Entities (DCCEs)**. The lifecycle progresses through **operational phases**, where the DT continuously collects real-time data (e.g., sensor inputs, operational metrics) and updates its digital representation to maintain **synchronization with the physical OME**. Transition management between phases is implicit in the ISO framework, emphasizing **modularity and interoperability** between entities (e.g., DTEs, DCCEs, User Entities) via standardized interfaces. Lifecycle governance is underpinned by the **ISO 23247 Part 2 reference architecture**, which mandates that each entity (e.g., DTE, DCCE) implements specific functionalities (e.g., data collection, user interaction) using **Functional Entities (FEs)**. While the standard does not prescribe implementation technologies (e.g., OPC UA is mentioned as an example), it ensures governance through **entity-based decomposition**, where each FE’s role (e.g., data transformation, human-machine interface) is explicitly defined. The lifecycle also implicitly includes **maintenance and evolution phases**, where the DT adapts to changes in the OME (e.g., upgrades, failures) via dynamic updates to the DTE’s digital representation. No explicit governance frameworks (e.g., ISO 55000 for asset management) are referenced in the provided documents, but compliance with ISO 23247 ensures alignment with manufacturing-specific lifecycle requirements.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **twinning process** and **Digital Twin (DT) evolution** in the context of **ISO 23247** are governed by an **entity-centric development methodology** where each **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)** is paired with a **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**. The process begins with **requirements elicitation**, where OMEs (e.g., machinery, processes) are identified and their observable attributes (e.g., operational states, sensor data) are defined. **Data Collection and Device Control Entities (DCCEs)** are then implemented to interface with the OME, collecting real-time or historical data via unspecified but standardized protocols (e.g., OPC Unified Architecture is cited as a potential implementation). The **DTE** is developed to mirror the OME’s physical and functional characteristics, leveraging **Functional Entities (FEs)** to handle tasks such as:
- **Data transformation** (e.g., converting raw sensor data into interpretable formats),
- **Actuation control** (e.g., sending commands to the OME),
- **User interaction** (e.g., providing dashboards or APIs for monitoring).

Quality assurance (QA) practices are implied through the **entity-based RA**, where each FE’s functionality is validated against its defined role (e.g., ensuring data accuracy for the DTE). Testing and validation are not explicitly detailed in the documents, but the modularity of FEs suggests **unit testing** for individual entities and **integration testing** for inter-entity communication (e.g., DTE ↔ DCCE). **Version control** is not addressed, but the standard’s emphasis on **modularity** implies that updates to the DT (e.g., evolving OMEs or FEs) can be managed via **incremental deployment** of updated entities. Evolution strategies are inferred from the **dynamic synchronization** requirement between DTEs and OMEs, where the DT adapts to changes in the physical system (e.g., equipment upgrades) through **reconfiguration of FEs** or **data model updates**. No explicit mention of Agile or DevOps methodologies is made, but the framework supports iterative development via its entity-based modularity.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The **insights and decision-making capabilities** of the Digital Twin (DT) system, as implied by **ISO 23247**, are derived from the **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**’s ability to process data collected by **Data Collection and Device Control Entities (DCCEs)**. The system generates **real-time and historical insights** by analyzing observable attributes of **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**, such as equipment performance, process efficiency, or operational anomalies. **Analytics and processing capabilities** are not explicitly detailed, but the DTE’s role suggests it employs **rule-based or lightweight computational models** to derive insights (e.g., predictive maintenance alerts, efficiency metrics). Decision-making processes are supported through **User Entities**, which provide interfaces (e.g., dashboards, APIs) for operators to interact with the DT. Users can query the DTE for **monitoring data**, **diagnostic reports**, or **control commands** (e.g., adjusting OME parameters). The confidence measures for recommendations are not specified, but the **synchronization between DTE and OME** implies that insights are grounded in real-time or near-real-time data, enhancing reliability. No advanced analytics (e.g., machine learning, simulation) are mentioned, but the modular **Functional Entities (FEs)** could theoretically integrate such capabilities. User interaction methods are limited to **human-machine interfaces (HMIs)** provided by User Entities, with no mention of collaborative tools or AI-driven assistants.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin (DT) system adheres to **ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing**, a **four-part standard** published in 2021. **Part 2 (Reference Architecture)** is the most relevant, defining an **entity-based RA** where:
- **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** (e.g., equipment, processes) are mapped to **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)**,
- **Data Collection and Device Control Entities (DCCEs)** handle bidirectional data/control flows,
- **User Entities** provide interfaces for interaction,
- **Functional Entities (FEs)** modularize tasks (e.g., data transformation, actuation).

The standard does not prescribe specific **certification requirements**, but compliance with **ISO 23247** ensures alignment with manufacturing DT best practices. **Interoperability standards** are implied through the RA’s modular design, with **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** mentioned as a potential protocol for FE implementation (e.g., DCCE ↔ OME communication). No other industry standards (e.g., IEC 62424 for energy systems, IEEE P2413 for IoT) are referenced. The framework emphasizes **modularity and interoperability** between entities, but does not mandate compliance with broader **interoperability frameworks** (e.g., Industry 4.0 or IIoT standards). While **Part 4 of ISO 23247** addresses information exchange, the provided documents do not detail specific **data formats, APIs, or semantic models** (e.g., ontologies) for cross-system communication.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The security and safety considerations for the Digital Twin system, as referenced in the provided documents, are primarily governed by **ISO 26262-1:2018 (Road vehicles – Functional safety)** and implicitly by **ISO 15622:2018 (Intelligent Transport Systems – Adaptive Cruise Control Systems)** for automotive and transport-related Digital Twin applications.

**Cybersecurity Protocols:**
- **ISO 26262-1:2018** mandates a **risk-based approach** to cybersecurity, requiring the implementation of **automotive-specific security measures** aligned with **ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level)** classifications. This includes **secure communication protocols** (e.g., **CAN FD, LIN, Ethernet**) with **cryptographic protection** (e.g., **AES-128, HMAC-SHA-256**) to prevent unauthorized access and tampering.
- **Access Control Mechanisms:** Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is implied for system components, ensuring only authorized entities (e.g., **ECUs, control units, or external actors**) interact with critical Digital Twin data. **Authentication Systems** must adhere to **ISO 26262-6** (if referenced) or similar standards, leveraging **digital certificates** and **secure boot processes** to validate system integrity.

**Threat Mitigation Strategies:**
- **Fail-Safe Mechanisms:** The system must incorporate **fail-operational** or **fail-safe designs** (e.g., **redundant sensors, watchdog timers**) to mitigate failures in real-time, as per **ISO 26262-5** (if applicable). For **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) systems** (ISO 15622), **emergency stop protocols** and **degraded-mode operation** are critical to prevent catastrophic failures.
- **Risk Assessment Approaches:** **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** and **FTA (Fault Tree Analysis)** are explicitly required by **ISO 26262-5** to identify and mitigate risks in Digital Twin components, particularly those interfacing with **safety-critical systems** (e.g., **brake control, collision avoidance**).

**Safety-Critical System Considerations:**
- The Digital Twin must align with **ISO 26262’s safety lifecycle**, including **requirements elicitation, design verification, and validation testing** for safety-related functions. **Model-in-the-Loop (MIL) and Software-in-the-Loop (SIL) testing** (as hinted in [37]) are essential to validate Digital Twin behavior under **extreme conditions** (e.g., **sensor failures, network latency**).
- **3GPP TS 22.185 (V2X services)** implies **secure data exchange** between Digital Twins and **roadside units (RSUs)** or **vehicles**, requiring **end-to-end encryption** and **authentication** to prevent spoofing or replay attacks in **cooperative Intelligent Transport Systems (C-ITS)**.

**Technical Specifications:**
- **Data Integrity:** Digital Twin data must be **immutable** for safety-critical operations, achieved via **blockchain-like ledgers** (if implied in [37]) or **cryptographic hashing** (e.g., **SHA-256**) to ensure consistency.
- **Latency Constraints:** For **real-time safety applications**, **ISO 15622** specifies **maximum response times** (e.g., **<100ms for ACC systems**), which must be enforced in the Digital Twin’s **control loop simulations**.

block_2_docs_retrieved:

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,
